name: french lentil salad
instructions:
Sauté leeks in olive oil over medium heat until softened. Add garlic if using. If going the white-wine-cream variant, deglaze with white wine here and let it cook off. Remove from heat and let the sauté cool.

In a bowl, combine cold cooked lentils with the chosen french vegetable (raw endives, or raw or shredded turnips/radishes — pick ONE).

Make the cream sauce by whisking heavy cream with the chosen acid: lemon juice, white wine, white wine vinegar, or dijon mustard.

Fold the cooled sautéed leeks into the lentil-and-vegetable bowl. Pour the cream sauce over and toss. Finish with the chosen nut (slivered almonds, toasted walnuts, or sunflower seeds).
notes:
THE STRUCTURE: raw lentils + sautéed leeks + french vegetable + nuts + cream sauce. Every component is in the dish; only some of them have fixed identities.

NON-FLEXIBLE (the rules):
- LEEKS IN OLIVE OIL, SAUTEED: the main flavor of the dish. Not negotiable.
- LENTILS COOKED AND COLD (NOT IN THE PAN): the lentils must be cooked, but they stay 'raw' in the sense that they never enter the sauté pan. Adding them to the pan is the trap — once they get mushy and heated, they start demanding stock, tarragon, and other stew-like vegetables, and the raw french vegetable and vinaigrette will clash with that direction. Keep them cold. Combine them with the cooled sauté and dressing off heat.
- HEAVY CREAM IN THE SAUCE: non-optional. The cream is what holds the dressing together against the lentils.
- NUTS: non-optional. They provide the textural crunch that the cold lentils don't.

FLEXIBLE (the variants):

CREAM SAUCE ACID — pick one:
- Lemon juice (fresh)
- White wine (fresh, cooked off with the leeks during the sauté)
- White wine vinegar
- Dijon mustard
Preference is for fresh acids (lemon or white wine), but white wine vinegar sometimes hits nicely depending on the day. Mustard cream is the more savory variant.

NUTS — pick one:
- Slivered almonds (default)
- Toasted walnuts
- Sunflower seeds
All work. Almonds are the canonical choice; the others are variety.

FRENCH VEGETABLE — pick ONE, never more:
- Endives (preferred — raw, cut)
- Turnips (raw or shredded)
- Radishes (raw or shredded)
The 'pick one' constraint is structural — these are all bitter vegetables, and combining two of them makes the dish overwhelmingly bitter (the cream sauce can balance one bitter element, not two). I prefer endives.

GARLIC: optional add during the leek sauté. Adds some sharpness; not necessary if the cream sauce is mustard-based (which already brings sharpness).

TARRAGON: unnecessary but nice. Stir a few chopped leaves into the cream sauce or the finished salad if you want the extra herbal voice. Most of the time the leeks and vegetable already carry enough green character.
